title: The 'ne' Rule (Ergative Past)
situation: Hindi's most-feared grammar rule: in the perfective past, TRANSITIVE verbs flip the construction. The subject gets 'ne' attached, and the verb agrees with the OBJECT, not the subject. 'maine khaaya' (I ate) — pure black magic until it clicks.

phrases.context: 'maine' = main + ne. 'pi' agrees with 'chai' (fem). Notice: NOT 'pee' (which would be masculine).
'usne' = us + ne. Verb 'padhi' agrees with 'kitaab' (fem). The subject's gender is IRRELEVANT — only the object matters.
'humne' = ham + ne. 'dekhi' agrees with 'movie' (fem in Hindi). Always check the OBJECT's gender first.
Contrast: 'jaana' is INTRANSITIVE → no 'ne'. Subject takes 'gayi' (fem, agrees with main = female speaker). Compare to the 'maine' phrases above.
Even with named subjects, attach 'ne'. 'banaya' (M) agrees with 'khaana' (M). If it had been 'sabzi' (F), would be 'banayi'.
Question form. 'tumne' = tum + ne. 'kaha' is a special case — fixed form, doesn't agree (because 'kya' doesn't have inherent gender).
'phone kiya' — 'kiya' agrees with 'phone' (M loanword). Even English loanwords get assigned a Hindi gender; phone is M.
'batayi' (F) — what's the object? Implicit 'baat' (story/thing, fem). The verb agrees with whatever was told, not the speaker or listener.
'rona' (to cry) is INTRANSITIVE — no 'ne', subject takes normal agreement. Compare with 'rulaya' (caused to cry, transitive) which would need 'ne'.

phrases.english: we got all the work done
phrases.context: Compound verb 'kar liya' (kar + le, perfective) — still triggers 'ne'. 'liya' agrees with 'kaam' (M). All compounds inherit the transitivity of their main verb.

grammar_notes: The 'ne' rule applies ONLY in the perfective past of TRANSITIVE verbs. Present, future, habitual, continuous past — no 'ne'.
Pronoun + 'ne' forms: main→maine, tu→tune, tum→tumne, aap→aapne, wo→usne (sg) / unhone (pl), ham→humne.
Verb agreement: with 'ne', the verb agrees with the DIRECT OBJECT (gender + number). NOT with the subject.
If the object also takes 'ko' (marking specificity), the verb stays in the default masculine-singular form. 'maine usko dekha' (I saw him/her) — 'dekha' is fixed.
Intransitive verbs (aana, jaana, baithna, sona, rona) NEVER take 'ne'. They use normal subject-verb agreement.
Compound verbs (kar liya, de diya, kha liya) inherit transitivity from the main verb. 'maine kar liya' (transitive → ne). 'mein so gayi' (intransitive → no ne).
culture_notes: Even native Hindi speakers occasionally trip over 'ne' in fast speech, especially in casual contexts. Getting it 80% right is good enough socially.
The 'ne' rule is one of the few grammar features that Bollywood films sometimes ignore for poetic effect — don't trust song lyrics as a reference.
Learners often over-apply 'ne' (using it in present/future) — be careful. It is ONLY for perfective past of transitive verbs.

skill_breakdown.skill: When to use 'ne' (transitive perfective past only)
skill_breakdown.explanation: Two conditions must BOTH be true: (1) verb is transitive (has a direct object), (2) tense is perfective past. Anywhere else, no 'ne'.

more_examples.hindi: main kitaab padhungi (✗ no ne — future)
main ghar gayi (✗ no ne — intransitive)

skill_breakdown.explanation: The verb agrees with the OBJECT (its gender + number), not the subject. Always check: what's the object's gender?

more_examples.english: I drank chai (verb fem to match chai)
I drank water (verb masc to match pani)

skill_breakdown.explanation: Some verbs are tricky: 'bolna' (speak) — transitive in Hindi → ne. 'rona' (cry) — intransitive → no ne. 'hasna' (laugh) — intransitive. When in doubt: does the verb take a direct object?

more_examples.english: I used to cry (also no ne in habitual, regardless)

practice_prompt: You are a Hindi grammar tutor specializing in the 'ne' rule. Give the user a short past-tense scenario and ask them to translate. They'll make mistakes — gently flag whether (a) they should/shouldn't have used 'ne', and (b) whether the verb agrees with the right gender. Use 'aap' (polite tutor).

theory.intro: This is the chapter Hindi learners fear most. The `ne` rule is the construction that makes English speakers say 'Hindi doesn't make sense' — but it actually does, you just have to flip your instincts. In past tense, for transitive verbs (verbs that take an object), the subject takes the postposition `ne` and the verb agrees with the OBJECT's gender, not the subject's. So `maine roti khaayi` ('I ate roti') uses the feminine verb ending because `roti` is feminine — even if the speaker is masculine. This is the single most distinctive feature of Hindi grammar; once you've drilled it, the rest of past tense locks into place.

sections.heading: What `ne` is and when it appears
sections.body:
`ne` is a tiny postposition that attaches to the SUBJECT of a sentence — but only in one very specific situation: when the verb is TRANSITIVE and the tense is SIMPLE PAST. Outside that combination, `ne` simply doesn't appear.

Think of `ne` as a flag that says 'a completed action acted on something'. In English you don't see this flag because the language hides it. In Hindi it's right there on the subject, in plain sight.

So: `main khaata hoon` (I eat — present, no `ne`), `main soya` (I slept — past, but intransitive, so no `ne`), `maine sandwich khaaya` (I ate a sandwich — past + transitive, so `ne` is required). The trigger is always the same: past tense + transitive verb.

examples.breakdown: present tense → no `ne` regardless of verb type
past tense BUT intransitive (sona = sleep) → no `ne`
past + transitive (khaana = eat takes an object) → `ne` required

callout.body: The two conditions must BOTH be true. Past alone isn't enough; transitive alone isn't enough. Only past + transitive together triggers `ne`.

sections.cutting_intro: Okay — deep breath. The `ne` rule has a reputation, but it's mechanical. Let's start with the trigger: when does `ne` even show up?

quick_check.question: Which sentence requires `ne`?
quick_check.explanation: Yes — `peena` (to drink) is transitive and the tense is past, so the subject gets `ne` (main → maine). The other two miss at least one of the two conditions.

sections.heading: Why this matters: the verb agrees with the OBJECT
sections.body:
Here's the headline consequence of `ne`, and the bit English speakers consistently miss: once `ne` shows up on the subject, the verb stops agreeing with the subject and starts agreeing with the OBJECT instead.

`maine roti khaayi` — `roti` is feminine, so the verb ends in `-i` (`khaayi`). `maine khaana khaaya` — `khaana` is masculine, so the verb ends in `-a` (`khaaya`). Same subject (`main` → `maine`), different object, completely different verb ending.

And crucially: the speaker's own gender doesn't matter. A female speaker says `maine paani piya` (paani is masculine, so `-a`), and a male speaker says `maine chai pi` (chai is feminine, so `-i`). The verb is locked to the object, not the speaker.

examples.breakdown: object = roti (f) → verb ends in -i, regardless of speaker gender
object = paani (m) → verb ends in -a, even if speaker is female
object = chai (f) → verb ends in -i, even if speaker is male
object = kitaabein (f. pl.) → verb ends in -i (historical irregularity — not -in)

callout.body: THIS is the rule English speakers forget. Your instinct will scream 'agree with the subject' because that's what English does. In a `ne` sentence, ignore the subject's gender entirely and track the OBJECT's gender. Re-train this reflex by drilling pairs aloud.

sections.cutting_intro: Here's the part everyone forgets. When `ne` shows up, the verb stops caring about the subject. It tracks the OBJECT instead.

quick_check.question: A female speaker says `maine paani _____ ` (drank). Which form is correct?
quick_check.explanation: Correct — `piya` (masculine). The verb agrees with `paani` (which is masculine), NOT with the speaker. Speaker gender is irrelevant in `ne` constructions.

sections.heading: Transitive vs intransitive — how to spot which
sections.body:
Since `ne` only attaches to transitive verbs, you need a reliable way to spot the difference. A transitive verb takes a direct object — the action acts ON something. Eat what? Read what? See who? Intransitive verbs don't — sleep, go, come, sit, cry. The action doesn't have a 'what'.

Some verbs sit in the middle. `bolna` (to speak) can lean either way depending on whether you specify what was said. Most everyday verbs are clearly one or the other — memorize the common transitive list below and the common intransitive list, and your instinct will sharpen fast.

table.caption: Common transitive (need `ne` in past) vs intransitive (never take `ne`)

callout.body: Quick test when in doubt: ask 'did the action act ON something?' If yes → transitive → `ne` required in past. If no → intransitive → never `ne`. Works 95% of the time.

sections.cutting_intro: Now — the trickiest piece. How do you know if a verb is transitive? Here's the cheat sheet.

quick_check.question: Which of these verbs is intransitive (never takes `ne`)?
quick_check.explanation: Yes — `sona` (to sleep) is intransitive. You don't sleep 'something'. Past tense: `main soya` / `main soyi`, no `ne` ever. The other two are transitive (you eat something, you write something).

sections.heading: The fused pronoun forms: maine / tune / usne / hamne
sections.body:
When `ne` attaches to a pronoun, the two don't stay separate — they fuse into a single word. So `main + ne` becomes `maine`, `tu + ne` becomes `tune`, `wo + ne` becomes `usne`, and so on. Even though it looks like the pronoun itself changed, it's really just pronoun + `ne` written as one word.

Memorize this table. These eight fused forms cover every subject pronoun in Hindi, and you'll use all of them constantly once you're past the basics.

examples.breakdown: wo + ne → usne (the pronoun shifts to oblique form before `ne`)

callout.body: Even though it looks like the pronoun itself transformed, remember: it's just pronoun + `ne` merged into one word. The 'shift' from `wo` → `us-` and `we` → `un-` is the same oblique form you see before any postposition (`usko`, `unke saath`, etc.).

sections.cutting_intro: `ne` doesn't sit separately after the pronoun — it fuses in. Learn these eight forms once and you've got every subject covered.

quick_check.question: How would you say 'we ate the food' using `ne`?
quick_check.explanation: Yes — `hamne khaana khaaya`. `ham + ne → hamne`. Verb is `khaaya` (masc.) because `khaana` (food) is masculine. The third option fails on the verb-agreement front.

sections.heading: The top mistakes English speakers make
sections.body:
Four classic traps. Drilling these in pairs — wrong form vs corrected form — is the fastest way to install the right reflex.

**Mistake 1: Forgetting `ne` entirely.** You'll default to subject-only because that's English. Wrong: `*main khaaya`. Right: `maine khaaya`.

**Mistake 2: Adding `ne` to intransitive verbs.** Wrong: `*main-ne soya` (sona is intransitive). Right: `main soya`.

**Mistake 3: Subject-agreement when object-agreement is required.** Wrong: `*maine roti khaaya` (masculine ending on a feminine object). Right: `maine roti khaayi`.

**Mistake 4: Using `ne` outside the simple past.** `ne` does NOT appear in present, future, or imperfective past. Wrong: `*main-ne khaata hoon`. Right: `main khaata hoon`.

examples.breakdown: Mistake 1: forgot `ne`. Past + transitive needs `ne` on the subject.
Mistake 2: added `ne` to intransitive `sona`. Sleep doesn't take an object — no `ne`.

examples.hindi: ✗ maine roti khaaya / ✓ maine roti khaayi
examples.breakdown: Mistake 3: verb agreed with the wrong noun. Object = roti (f), so ending is -i, not -a.

examples.hindi: ✗ main-ne khaata hoon / ✓ main khaata hoon
examples.breakdown: Mistake 4: `ne` in present tense. `ne` is only for simple past. Present always uses bare subject.

callout.body: Drill in pairs. Say a past intransitive (`main soya`) followed by a past transitive (`maine khaana khaaya`) out loud, back to back. Your ear will start flagging the wrong shape automatically — that's the goal.

sections.cutting_intro: Last stop — let's look at the four mistakes English speakers make over and over. Spot them now, save yourself months of corrections.

quick_check.question: Which sentence is CORRECT?
options: maine roti khaaya (masc. verb + fem. object)
quick_check.explanation: Yes — only the third is correct. `padhna` is transitive and we're in past tense, so `ne` is required (`main → maine`). `kitaab` is feminine, so verb ends in `-i`. The first wrongly adds `ne` to intransitive `jaana`; the second has masc. verb on a fem. object.

theory.wrap_up: The `ne` rule is hard, but it's mechanical once internalized: SIMPLE PAST + TRANSITIVE = subject takes `ne` and verb agrees with the object. Master the trigger first ('am I in past tense with a transitive verb?'), then the fused pronoun forms (`maine`, `tune`, `usne`...), then practice the verb agreement with the OBJECT. Get past this one and you're past the hardest grammar wall in Hindi. The phrases below give pair-drill practice across transitive and intransitive past sentences — say them aloud, back to back, until the right shape feels automatic.
